turns.user_message: Honestly, I don't know where to start. I've been smoking for 20 years, and I've tried to quit three times before. The last time was six months ago, and I lasted for three months before I started again. It's just so hard.

past_strategies.outcome: lasted for 3 months before started again

step2_rag_chunks: Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
Avoid keeping cigarettes, lighters, or ashtrays nearby when trying to quit smoking.

relevant_strategies.outcome: lasted for 3 months before started again

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal

Therapist: I can imagine. It takes a lot of courage to try to quit, and it's not easy. What made you want to quit smoking in the first place?

Patient: Well, my doctor's been on my case about it, and I know it's bad for my health. But to be honest, I'm also worried about how it's affecting my mental health. I've been struggling with depression and anxiety, and I'm worried that quitting will make it worse.

Therapist: I understand. It's great that you're aware of the connection between smoking and your mental health. Can you tell me more about what you mean by "make it worse"? What are your specific concerns?

Patient: Well, I've been prescribed medication, but I'm not always good about taking it. And when I'm feeling anxious or stressed, smoking helps me calm down. I'm worried that if I quit, I'll just end up using more marijuana to cope, and that's not good for me either.

Therapist: I see. It sounds like you're worried about replacing one coping mechanism with another. That's a valid concern. Have you considered other ways to manage your stress and anxiety that don't involve substances?
Patient: Like what? I've tried meditation and deep breathing, but it doesn't work for me. And my friends and family don't get why I want to quit, so I don't have a lot of support.
